title: Irreversibility

content.statement: Decisions vary in how easily they can be reversed; treat them differently.
content.question: Is this decision a one-way or two-way door, and am I treating it accordingly?
content.mental_model: Door, some swing back, some lock behind you.
content.business_lens: Categorize decisions as reversible (move fast) vs irreversible (gather data, deliberate). Avoid mixing the two.
content.life_lens: Distinguish which choices in your life can be undone and which cannot; allocate care accordingly.
sop: Categorize the decision as reversible or irreversible
For reversible: act fast, learn quickly
For irreversible: gather more data, take more time
Watch for false irreversibility (often less binding than feared)
Avoid treating reversible decisions as if they were irreversible, it slows the company
content.explainer: Bezos argued most decisions in business are reversible (two-way doors) and should be made fast. Treating them as irreversible slows the company. Conversely, true one-way doors (acquisitions, public commitments, key hires) deserve slowness and rigor.
questions: Is this decision a one-way or two-way door, and am I treating it accordingly?
What decision have you spent weeks deliberating on that you could actually reverse in a day if it went wrong?
What decision did you make quickly last month that you actually can't take back, a hire, a public statement, a contract? Would you have moved that fast if you'd paused to categorize it first?
content.equation_example: Rate Irreversibility 1-10 (10 equals totally permanent): 3, mostly reversible, so 1 divided by 3, approximately 0.33. Information Quality rated 8 out of 10. Score = 0.33 times 8, approximately 2.7. Higher scores mean move fast, the decision is reversible and you have good information; lower scores, high irreversibility paired with weak information, mean slow down and gather more before committing.
content.closing_statement: Reversible calls deserve speed; permanent ones deserve patience.

caption:
Principle Nº 324: Irreversibility

Decisions vary in how easily they can be reversed; treat them differently. Bezos argued most decisions in business are reversible (two-way doors) and should be made fast.

Ask yourself: Is this decision a one-way or two-way door, and am I treating it accordingly?
